sentences.text: My name is Rose Huppert.
And my maiden name?
Originally Zarasinska.
And I'll spell it-- Z, like zebra, A-R-A-S-I-N-S-K-A, Zarasinska.
And my married name is Huppert, H-U- double P like Peter, E-R-T, Huppert.
No.
My date of birth is August 14, 1926.
Well I guess I graduated already seventh grade I'm in-- [INAUDIBLE]. 70 I'm going to be now.
Sos-- I was born in Poland.
And the city-- Sosnowiec.
S-O-S-N-O-W-I and C-E, Sosnowic-- without the E, actually, but it called [?
Sosnowiec. ?] But Sosnowiec is without the E.
Do you need the address too?
OK.
My earliest memories at-- at home?
Well, I was-- I grew up in a home with love, respect, culture, true tradition.
And I was taught respect for everyone to each other.
And my parents did everything to their-- their priority was the children, education.
And they did the utmost to give the best for the children, and helped others, naturally.
Can I continue, or what?
I was always-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well we-- how do I say it?
Well, do you want to know how it was in school, or at home, or what?
Well, firstly, why don't you tell me about your family, about your mother and father.
About my family?
All right, my Daddy's name was Abraham, Avram Zarasinski.
My mom was Rivka Zarasinska.
And her maiden name was Rivka Gold-- maiden name.
And they been-- mom was born in [?
Slavkov. ?] And they when all-- and they've been business people.
My daddy was-- they were in business.
The whole family always successful business people.
And then we owned a wholesale and retail of meats.
And also we owned-- we employed people.
And we owned-- we had a-- owned a building, which people were employed, regardless with-- in business also.
Our-- what our home-- we had a administrator what he took care of the taxes, and books, and the tenants to collect tax-- I mean, the rent.
And his name was Auerbach, Milo Auerbach.
And then we had also a house-- woman, which is they call a [POLISH] in Polish.
But is like-- I think in English it's more like a housekeeper, outside, to keep clean, and to keep the light, the door-- the check for the tenants.
And we had-- and also a sleeping and maid, which she stayed with us [INAUDIBLE] and at home.
And our pride was the name.
The name was-- I owe this my parents.
I'm telling this to the world, to verify they were just wonderful, great human beings, not just a-- not just a statistic or a number.
And that was we were proud of the name, like a crown on their heads, which they was crushed and turned to ashes.
And what hurts the most-- that without scruples-- you know, too many coming already up, denying that this tragedy and Holocaust exists.
And I've--
Then we had-- I was-- we used to go on vacation.
I had in the mountains.
We had the wonderful-- and we taught-- my parents taught us music.
And I played mandolin.
And my-- there was tutor, which told us-- my Daddy spoke five languages.
And that was Hebrew, Jewish, Polish, German, and Russia-- Russian language.
And Hebrew-- the Bible, he translated with every-- like, if you know what the-- the Torah has the Hebrew, and the dots, he was able to transfer every dot, the meaning of it.
He was very knowledgeable.
So was-- my mom was very intelligent.
My sister was very bright.
My brother was-- like I said, my parents always to put the priority to send to the best school.
And he was going to one of-- private school, one of the highest rated schools in the country.
It was the [?
Yavna ?] [?
Fistenburg. ?] And after that, he will attend to engineering school, or [SPEAKING POLISH], in Katowice, which there Jews were not permitted to attend.
But as a non-Jew-- we had a client which she was the director of the colleges.
And she was in contact.
So she tried to-- because I mean, we had a Polish name, Zarasinski, she told them, [?
Josef ?] Zarasinski, [?
Joseph ?] Zarasinski.
That's the way she called.
And she-- that's the way he got into this college, engineering.
And natur-- and there was another Jew, also.
But they did not know he was Jewish-- [?
Lonav ?] Davinsky.
They were the only two which attended school.
And he got his diploma just before the war.
And naturally then, by the end, when they-- he got the diploma, they revealed-- that realized that he was Jewish.
But it was too late.
They had to give him the diploma.
And--
My sister's name was Frania.
She was the most beautiful child-- intelligent, as young as she was.
And she was like a tomboy-- beautiful green eyes.
Everybody loved her.
And in fact, and when I was in-- in 1989, we'd been visiting with my husband in Israel, the gathering for Sosnowiec, from our town.
And we had to-- we wore our name on the blouse, or whatever we wear.
And some lady approached me with her granddaughter.
And she looked at the name.
She says, you know, she-- and I quote it she said to the granddaughter.
You know, I-- this lady's sister, Frania-- she was not only the best student from the class, but from the whole school.
And she used to sing well.
And she do some drawing.
And then she looked at me.
And she looked a little bit like you.
[LAUGHS] And anyway, everybody loved her.
And that was about-- my parents friends-- anytime we went, they been all respected by everyone-- Gentile, non-Jew.
And we learned the same way to respond to people.
To us, that's the way we were taught, to respect others.
And-- and education, wisdom-- that's what we learn at home.
And tradition-- every shabbat-- I mean, my parents were very dedicated to Judaism.
And we had also-- my parents also spent a lot-- they fund philanthropic causes for sick people, for they call [NON-ENGLISH].
And my daddy also-- they married some orphan brides, helped to marry them off.
And my Daddy also dedicated a part of our apartment, a part which made like a prayer place, which the people came and did their daily prayers, which they call a [NON-ENGLISH] sort of.
And my brother, who was very talented, he made the of the ark beautiful two lions.
They looked like real.
He painted them and-- which they were holding the Ten Commandments.
And, as a teenager-- he was 13 or 14 years old-- for fun, he made a-- a camera.
What do you call it?
A movie projector, which they showed us films, old films, which was so [INAUDIBLE].
And we also had a tutor who taught us music.
And I was singing, [LAUGHS] which they knew.
And in school, I was very active.
I was participating in many school plays and recitations.
And also exercised with the whole school.
And I was very-- I think, considered us [INAUDIBLE].
I tried to be active, and as much as I draw things, and so forth, and participate in the choir.
And then I also-- that was our-- I would-- that was I-- childhood, I used to go with friends, and so forth.
And we had wonderful time spending.

And matter of fact, somebody remind me, when I was in Israel, of somebody whom-- our neighbors, accidentally which I met in Israel-- she remind me how wonderful it was to visit, how much they loved to come to our backyard, to the garden.
And-- and she asked me if I still have this beautiful voice, like a nightingale.
I say, no, it's gone.
[LAUGHS] Anyway.
Yes, that's what I wanted to mention, shabbat.
Friday night, there was tradition.
Daddy always wear this little hat, the [NON-ENGLISH].
Every day, he wore the hat.
And he did not shave.
But on the Friday night, he just put on a certain special coat.
It was with a sash, a purple-- a maroon sash.
And we had always somebody to come, unfortunate-- the less fortunate people to be invited to-- for supper.
On the holidays-- matter of fact, a year before the war started, we had people.
We brought a cantor from Pils.
And that was a cantor and the son.
And they were guests in our home for the high holy days.
For Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, they brought in special people to pray, with good voices, and with better knowledge-- whatever.
My parents, they were like-- Daddy was-- he was like a modern orthodox.
He was to God and to people.
And mom was, as well, and very intelligent.
And she helped others, as well-- needy, hungry.
She always used to send me with things.
It was anonymously, so would not-- no one should know, or or that somebody know about it-- always quiet, discrete, very discrete.
Never talked about it.
Matter of fact, the same thing happened to my grandmother.
And during-- can I say how-- no.
Maybe during the war you want to tell that, that after she passed away, that was-- and she got a heart attack when a German soldier went-- jumped into the window.
And my grandma had a coffee.
And she  got a heart attack from fear.
And that time-- that was in the beginning of the war.
And mom-- they told-- when they went-- came back from funeral, that there were a lot-- well, the people come and cried.
Sara, ?] who is going to take care of us?
They have always been very generous and caring, the whole family.
And, as matter of fact, they also-- when my grandfather passed away, blessed memory.
So she devoted her chandelier that looked very colorful.
I don't remember-- like in a-- like in a very vivid-- I mean, actually, like in a dream, because it's-- I'd just been very young then.
But I recall, like the colors, like have you seen those Tiffany's lights, lamps?
The colors were beautiful.
And my husband, when he went to visit after the war, with his wife and the youngest son, which he wanted to go back and see his-- where we came from.
So this chandelier-- amazing, surprisingly-- was still hanging in the synagogue.
Now, before the war, like I said, we-- business and my-- and everything was very successful, and a happy home.
And just before the war, we'd started-- anti-Semitism-- after the Marshall [INAUDIBLE] passed away, anti-Semitism started to grow and came more to the surface.
And they start to-- their slogans, anti-Semitic slogans, and boycotting, boycotting, boycotting the Jewish stores, and so forth.
And my sister was standing, matter of fact, by-- not far from this store, on this street, on her skates.
And a Pole-- she slipped and fell down.
And a Pole passing to work-- he just deliberately stepped on her leg and broke her leg.
And then my parents started to like to sell the home and try to emigrate, but it was too much already pressure, that they knew that we wouldn't be able to sell the house.
If they did want it, probably for nothing.
So it was difficult.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what can I say?

I think I have picture what I mentioned to you, when I was in Israel, visiting somebody had-- a friend of mine, which she was-- I was very surprised that she was willing to-- I think, give up her picture and give it to me.
She brought it to me and just reminisce, and to show the teachers we had, and the whole gang, the class which we participated together.
So-- Before the war, you know, when you were still a little girl, and, you know, 10, 11, 12 years old, tell me what Sosnowiec was like?
Sosnowiec--
--was a great community.
And we actually lived in the suburb, [?
Shelsa. ?] This is a little bit-- it was more-- it's a mixed-- actually Jewish and Polish, mixed-- what do you call?
Can you help me out?
A mixed--
Yeah, a community, right.
They were-- but a lot of business people [INAUDIBLE].
And they all kind of-- you find the well situated and less fortunate.
But we had about 35,000 people in Sosnowiec.
It was a big Jewish community.
And they been-- as a child, I didn't know much, probably, about the life.
Because you live always in your own circle, your own-- you have your own clique.
You use your own friends, going to school, and so forth.
So I-- actually, but I know there was a very pulse-- pulsing city, with life, young people there.
It was beautiful.
I think that was the most beautiful generation of the young people.
There was the-- there was intelligence.
You have intellectual people, and the intellect-- how do you call it?
Intelligentsia, which was wiped out.
The most beautiful youth.
Just before the war, the [? young ?] was just beautiful.
And it seems everything was destroyed by Hitler.
We had this-- excuse me.
It was sadly to say that Hitler destroyed the culture, the tradition, everything we loved so much, the Intelligentsia, the youth, even the baby didn't have a chance to bloom, to blossom.
We were cut off, completely.
We had not even teenage life.

Because there was just-- just starting to open our eyes to look at the world, where we can completely cut off and destroyed, and turned to numbers, with people with no name, no kin, just living on terror and-- and fear.
Yes--
I'm sorry.
Josef.
Chaim Josef, yeah.
Well, holidays-- at holiday, in holiday shabbat, we always-- traditional way of-- way of living, which they always observed all the [NON-ENGLISH], and all the laws of the-- our religion requires.
And we had also fun.
I mean, our [INAUDIBLE] was small.
We tried to make-- with our neighbors, [? maids, ?] and friends, we tried-- my brother always created.
We tried to make shows.
And they get together and bake something like this, and play, and sing, and make a show, which they had to pay tickets.
And for this money, we got something to share between the kids.
And mainly, it was-- I even-- when I would play mandolin, whatever one could.
And how we did enjoy it.
Well, like I-- like I said, that was-- that's the way we could make the best of it.
And we go to movie, or the parents took us, because we weren't allowed to go to any movie without escorting.
And books-- we weren't allowed only certain books.
That was always a limit for that.
But we had our stories and traditions.
And we read-- we read very-- later on, I read very important artists.
Like I used to like good books, like Dickens, and Dumas, and Raymond, and [?
Vassamun ?].
I hated Tolstoy.
[LAUGHS] And [INAUDIBLE] is one thing I hated in school.
But otherwise, we do enjoy literature.
And my parents always very knowledgeable and otherwise try to teach our children to use as much-- to learn as much as you can.
And we had friends, which they were deported.
And some of them tried to copy, [LAUGHS] to be [INAUDIBLE].
And we've been very close to each other.
And-- and I would say-- no.
No, we did not-- I tried, in the beginning-- we used to have some-- before we bought our home, we lived in an apartment with the Jews and-- practically we'd been the only practically Jew there.
Across the street, we had-- which they'd been very close with my parents.
They're businesspeople also.
And one of the sons, what I mentioned-- one man who-- young man who went to school T my brother, to engineering, after he became a fiance with one of the girls later on.
Bundt-- the name was Bundt.
And they got engaged with [?
Lonav ?] Davinsky.
[INAUDIBLE] they were already older than we were.
That was just before the war.
And we used to have fun with them.
And the parents used to associate and play [INAUDIBLE] whatever they get together with each other.
And family-- we used to get very close tie with our family, the cousins.
The worst part is, when it came passover, I had all the beautiful clothes, and I had to stay home, because I always got sick.
That seems like it was terrific timing.
My grandma, [INAUDIBLE], she was over.
And she came to visit when my grandfather passed away.
She used to bring the [INAUDIBLE] to the children.
They all-- she was always treated as a-- she was very gracious lady.
And she was always treated like a princess.
She never did anything, not like I.
Everything I do for the grandchildren.
She wouldn't-- no comparison.
I think there was a different way of living.
I think now we live different.
That time was different.
We have respect.
We have-- I didn't went to school.
If I had to go to school, I have to kiss my daddy and my mom in the hand before I left.
Otherwise, I thought the sky will fall over me.
And then school, like I said, we try always our best.
And our parents were very proud.
And I think everybody tried to look up to, and to be like them, like parents with their-- that's what in their pride, I think, that which every parents, I assume, is the same way.
And our cousins-- like I say, we've been together.
We'd play together.
And we see each other in time.
I had a-- we had also communicate together-- how do you say-- with them.
Most of them been our age.
So it was easy to communicate.
And we had our friends, which they-- we'd like to be with them.
And what else can I say?
When did things start to change in Sosnowiec?

Well, just like I say, just before the war, when it start to get very tense-- the situation-- and my mom-- just shortly before the war, they were talking-- they tried to cover from us-- is it  time to change?
They tried to whisper-- they whispered.
They didn't want the children to know everything.
So naturally, I wasn't informed about anything.
But Grandpa-- Daddy always read [INAUDIBLE] the papers.
Anyway, they'd send us out.
They sent me and my sister, and their aunts, and their younger two boys and girls to my uncle, to [?
Slawkow ?], where my mom came from, to my uncle-- try to avoid from anything.
Because they heard the Germans, the war, was coming.
And day it can break out.
So we went.
They sent us out there.
And we stayed with the uncle, with my grandma and the whole family, with their children.
And that's how we remained.
My parents remained with my brother in Sosnowiec, in hiding.
They went into some distant relatives, which they also had grown up boys.
So they thought, for time being, they are safe.
And the first time, when finally the Germans marched in, after I came back with my sister, we found out that was the first city, the first [INAUDIBLE], the first Jews killed when they marched in.
That's very peculiar, because [INAUDIBLE] street was so narrow.
And I don't know why they went particularly to this section.
That was downtown.
Hudna, they called.
And they killed 30 Jews.
And one of them-- two of them were from our tenants.
One was Schwartzman, a young couple, just got married.
And the other one was the sister-in-law.
The brother also lived there-- was [?
Alterwein. ?] They had two small children.
So two men that's from our house-- they got killed also.
And later, when they-- we came back, I heard, I think, my grandma-- that before I left of my grandmother, I never seen her crying.
But I think like, she felt that it would be probably the last time I seen her.
Her tears are coming down from the cheek.
And I didn't-- I think when you're young, you don't even realize the situation, how dangerous, how serious it is.
And we just-- everything-- you just follow, like a-- didn't-- like a-- they didn't-- nobody realizes something that [INAUDIBLE].
But then we start to hear all kind of stories, which they always hide it from us.
When we came back, I heard this tragedy.
And grandma, being at by my uncle-- because she had her home, but the situation-- obviously, she came to have-- drink coffee or something.
And one German jumped into the window.
And she got scared.
And she got a heart attack drinking her coffee.
That's what I found out from I-- and that's what I say, when I mentioned it before, how people were sorry and cried when she passed away.
And then also, after that, my daddy-- he tried to-- the life turned to horror.
I mean, in the beginning, we didn't know as much.
They told us to wear a white band with a star.
That time, we start to be marked like a-- as a-- only one word, for crime, something we never commit, being a Jew, [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we really start to say-- and I remember that in the beginning, when they gave us some leaflet, which being a youngster, I didn't realize, literally, what this-- what the words meant.
There are so many countries, so much-- so many lands.
And for us, there's no room.
To us, we just repeated, like a-- something we couldn't observe in our mind, that something could be so strange to us.
Finally, when they start-- and every day, they start to hear-- they systematically [INAUDIBLE].
Sometimes, they grabbed young people.
Sometimes boys from the street-- they disappeared.
They didn't know where they went.
So naturally, the parents start to worry.
And they start to-- every day, somewhere else to try to hide.
So I was one night-- first of all, about my brother.
